---
title: "3.3.4 Virtual memory"
subtitle: "Hardware P129-P131"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.24em; }
        .reveal .figure-note { font-size: 0.82em; line-height: 1.28; }
        .reveal .clean-table table { font-size: 0.76em; line-height: 1.1; }
        .reveal .clean-table th, .reveal .clean-table td { padding: 0.24em 0.32em; vertical-align: top; }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 3 Directory / 返回 Unit 3 目录](../../index.html)

## 3.3.4 Virtual memory

### Why it is needed

One of the problems associated with memory management is the case when processes run out of <span class="term">RAM</span>.

If the amount of available RAM is exceeded due to multiple programs running, it is likely to cause a <span class="mark">system crash</span>.

This can be solved by utilising the hard disk drive (or <span class="term">SSD</span>) if we need more memory. This is the basis behind <span class="term">virtual memory</span> (虚拟内存).

Essentially, RAM is the <span class="mark">physical memory</span>, while virtual memory is <span class="mark">RAM + swap space on the hard disk or SSD</span>.

## Figure 3.68

### Without virtual memory

Suppose we have five programs (numbered 0 to 4) that are in memory, all requiring access to RAM.

![Figure 3.68 Normal memory management](../../assets/3-hardware/3-3-4-virtual-memory-p129-p131/Figure 3.68 Normal memory management.png){fig-align="center" width="88%"}

When program <span class="mark">4</span> tries to access RAM there is no available memory, causing a <span class="mark">system crash</span>.

## Figure 3.69

### Status just before program 4 is given RAM space

To allow all five programs to access RAM as required, data is moved out of RAM into HDD/SSD and then other data is moved out of HDD/SSD into RAM.

![Figure 3.69 Status just before program 4 is given RAM space](../../assets/3-hardware/3-3-4-virtual-memory-p129-p131/Figure 3.69 Status just before program 4 is given RAM space.png){fig-align="center" width="90%"}

Notice the use of the <span class="mark">32-bit map</span>. When a program in memory tries to access RAM, it is sent to the map, which then allocates the program address space in RAM.

Program <span class="mark">4</span> cannot use RAM yet and its data has been mapped to the HDD/SSD instead because program <span class="mark">0</span> is the oldest data.

## Figure 3.70

### Status with program 0 mapped to HDD/SSD

Virtual memory now moves the <span class="mark">oldest data</span> out of RAM into the HDD/SSD to allow program 4 to gain access to RAM.

![Figure 3.70 Status with program 0 now mapped to HDD and program 4 has access to RAM](../../assets/3-hardware/3-3-4-virtual-memory-p129-p131/Figure 3.70 Status with program 0 now mapped to HDD and program 4 has access to RAM.png){fig-align="center" width="90%"}

The map is updated to reflect the new situation:

::: {.tight-list}
- data from program <span class="mark">0</span> is now mapped to the HDD/SSD instead
- address space <span class="mark">3</span> is left free for use by program 4
- program <span class="mark">4</span> now maps to address space 3 in RAM
:::

Virtual memory gives the <span class="mark">illusion of unlimited memory</span> being available.

## Paging, Benefits and Drawbacks

### What the textbook emphasises

In computer operating systems, <span class="term">paging</span> (分页) is used by memory management to store and retrieve data from HDD/SSD and copy it into RAM.

A <span class="term">page</span> is a <span class="mark">fixed-length consecutive block of data</span> utilised in virtual memory systems.

The main benefits of virtual memory are:

::: {.tight-list}
- programs can be larger than physical memory and still be executed
- there is no need to waste memory with data that is not being used
- it reduces the need to buy and install more expensive RAM memory
:::

However, accessing data in virtual memory is <span class="mark">slower</span>, so the larger the RAM the faster the CPU can operate.

## Disk Thrashing

### The main drawback

When using HDD for virtual memory the main drawback is <span class="term">disk thrashing</span> (磁盘抖动).

As main memory fills, more and more data needs to be swapped in and out of virtual memory leading to a very high rate of hard disk read/write head movements.

If more and more time is spent moving data in and out of memory than actually doing any processing, then the processing speed of the computer will be considerably reduced.

A point can be reached when the execution of a process comes to a halt since the system is so busy moving data in and out of memory rather than doing any actual execution. This is known as the <span class="mark">thrash point</span>.

Thrashing can be reduced by:

::: {.tight-list}
- installing <span class="mark">more RAM</span>
- reducing the number of programs running at a time
- reducing the size of the <span class="term">swap file</span> (交换文件)
- making use of a <span class="term">solid state drive (SSD)</span> rather than using HDD
:::

## Classroom Check

### Keep virtual memory tied to the textbook model

A complete answer should include:

::: {.tight-list}
- that virtual memory is <span class="mark">RAM + swap space on the hard disk or SSD</span>
- that virtual memory avoids a <span class="mark">system crash</span> when RAM is exceeded
- that the textbook model uses a <span class="mark">map</span> to show which program is in RAM and which is on HDD/SSD
- that the <span class="mark">oldest data</span> can be moved out of RAM to make space
- that <span class="term">paging</span> uses fixed-length blocks called <span class="term">pages</span>
- that <span class="term">disk thrashing</span> is the main drawback when too much swapping happens
:::

## Bridge

### Next: 3.3.5 Cloud storage

The next deck moves from virtual memory to <span class="term">cloud storage</span>.

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 3 Directory / 返回 Unit 3 目录](../../index.html)
